title: Needlestick Injury & BBV Exposure
oneLiner: Occupational or accidental puncture with a sharps device contaminated with blood/body fluids, requiring urgent risk assessment and post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) to prevent HIV, HBV, and HCV infection.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Missed 72-hour window for HIV PEP:** The single most critical error. If the injury is high-risk (deep, visible blood, hollow-bore needle from known HIV+ source), delaying PEP beyond 72 hours makes it ineffective. *Finding: injury >72 hours old or source status unknown – do not defer PEP while awaiting results; start empiric PEP if high-risk.*
- **Acute fulminant hepatitis B (rare in first hour but rapid in non-immune):** No immediate killer, but the failure to give HBV vaccine + HBIG within 48 hours can lead to seroconversion and chronic disease. *Finding: non-immune staff (anti-HBs <10 IU/L) with HBsAg+ source needle injury – treat as time-critical (ideally within 24h).*
- **Anaphylaxis to PEP drugs (very rare):** First dose of raltegravir or tenofovir can cause hypersensitivity. *Finding: urticaria, angioedema, hypotension within minutes of first PEP dose – stop drug, call ED.*
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Anxiety, fear, guilt (most prominent – do not dismiss), wound site pain/bleeding, history of sharps injury (time, device type, source patient details)
- **Key exam findings:** Puncture wound (size, depth, location – e.g. finger, palm, through glove), visible blood on needle, wound erythema (if late presentation), check source patient notes if available (HIV/HBV/HCV status, viral loads)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Needlestick injury with BBV exposure** – history of percutaneous injury with blood/body fluid contact; no other plausible cause for the event.
- **Needlestick injury without exposure** – injury from a closed system, sterile needle, or dry blood (non-infectious); differentiate by asking: "Was there visible blood on the needle? Was the source known? Was the needle hollow-bore?"
- **Mucocutaneous exposure** – splash to eyes, mouth, or non-intact skin; same BBV risk but lower transmission probability; different management (no PEP for HIV if low risk, but HBV/HCV same).
- **Splitter:** **Depth of injury + visible blood** – a deep puncture with bloody hollow-bore needle = high risk (treat as PEP-eligible); a superficial scratch with a solid needle (e.g. suture needle) without visible blood = very low risk (no PEP needed, but still test source).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **Immediate first aid:** Wash wound with soap and water (do not scrub), encourage bleeding (do not suck wound), cover with waterproof dressing.
  - **Assess wound:** depth, location, device type (hollow-bore vs solid), presence of blood.
  - **Source patient risk assessment:** Check notes / lab results for known HIV, HBV, HCV, syphilis. If source unknown (e.g. public sharps bin), treat as high risk.
- **Bloods (baseline for clinician):**
  - **HIV serology** (with consent, document “baseline status”)
  - **HBsAg, anti-HBs, anti-HBc** (to check immunity)
  - **Anti-HCV** (baseline)
  - **Syphilis serology** (if high risk)
  - **LFTs** (baseline for PEP monitoring)
  - **Routine FBC, U&Es** (if PEP to be started)
- **Imaging:** None.
- **Special tests:**
  - **Source patient testing:** Only if they consent – test for HIV (p24 antigen + antibody), HBsAg, HCV antibody. Do not delay PEP awaiting results.
  - **Tetanus status:** Check; give tetanus booster if >10 years since last dose (or tetanus immunoglobulin if high-risk wound + unknown vaccination).
- **Never-miss test:** **Baseline HIV test** – required before starting PEP to avoid treating unrecognised pre-existing infection (false attribution of seroconversion to the injury). **Anti-HBs** – if <10 IU/L, they are non-immune and need HBV PEP.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **First aid** (as above) – do before any other step.
  - **Risk stratification:** Use local policy (e.g. UKAP / NICE algorithm). Key variables:
    - Source: known HIV+ / HBsAg+ / HCV+ or unknown
    - Injury: deep, hollow-bore, visible blood → high risk
    - Clinician immunity: HBV vaccinated (anti-HBs >10) vs non-immune
  - **HIV PEP:** If high risk (deep injury + source HIV+ OR unknown + patient request), start **as soon as possible, within 72 hours**. *Do not wait for source test results if clinical concern.*
    - Drugs: tenofovir disoproxil + emtricitabine (Truvada) 1 tablet daily **plus** raltegravir 400mg twice daily (or dolutegravir 50mg daily) for 28 days. [Check local guideline for exact combination and dosing; some use bictegravir/emtricitabine/tenofovir alafenamide single tablet]
  - **HBV PEP:**
    - If **non-immune** (anti-HBs <10): give **HBIG 500 IU IM** (ideally within 24–48h) **plus** start HBV vaccine (Engerix B 20mcg IM at 0, 1, 2, 6 months).
    - If **immune** (anti-HBs >10): no additional prophylaxis needed.
  - **HCV:** No PEP available. Baseline serology and monitor at 6 weeks, 12 weeks, 6 months.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Admit if:** high-risk injury where PEP compliance is uncertain, severe anxiety, or need for IV PEP (rare – e.g. unable to tolerate oral).
  - **Counselling:** Discuss transmission risks (HIV ~0.3%, HBV ~30% if HBeAg+, HCV ~3%), PEP side effects (nausea, fatigue, renal impairment), and safe sexual practices (condom use, avoid blood donation) until follow-up negative serology.
  - **Follow-up schedule:** HIV test at 6 weeks, 12 weeks, 6 months; HCV test at 6 weeks, 12 weeks, 6 months; LFTs monthly while on PEP (if starting tenofovir, check renal function at baseline and 4 weeks).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Occupational health referral** (mandatory) – they will manage the full follow-up, psychological support, and documentation.
  - **Complete HBV vaccine course** if started (0,1,2,6 months).
  - **If source develops new infection** (e.g. delayed positive HCV), recall exposed clinician for further testing.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: nil specific (normal diet).
  - VTE prophylaxis: not routinely indicated unless immobile.
  - Observations: baseline vital signs only (not for monitoring infection – for PEP side effects if severe).
  - NBM: not required.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **HIV PEP (first-line):**
  - Tenofovir disoproxil 245mg + emtricitabine 200mg (Truvada) **1 tablet once daily** **PLUS** raltegravir 400mg **twice daily** (or dolutegravir 50mg **once daily**) for 28 days. [If using a single-tablet regimen like Biktarvy (bictegravir/emtricitabine/tenofovir alafenamide), follow local policy]
  - **Alternative:** If renal impairment (eGFR <60), use tenofovir alafenamide based regimen (e.g. Descovy) – consult pharmacist.
  - **Key contraindications:** Pre-existing renal impairment (check eGFR before starting); pregnancy (raltegravir or dolutegravir are safe, but tenofovir disoproxil can be used with monitoring).
- **HBV PEP:**
  - **Hepatitis B immunoglobulin (HBIG):** 500 IU IM once (within 48h, ideally 24h). No known contraindications.
  - **Hepatitis B vaccine (Engerix B):** 20mcg IM at 0, 1, 2, 6 months.
  - **Contraindications:** Hypersensitivity to yeast; but if non-immune, benefit outweighs risk.
- **Tetanus prophylaxis:**
  - Tetanus vaccine (if >10 years since last booster) – 0.5ml IM.
  - Tetanus immunoglobulin (if wound high-risk + unknown vaccination) – 250 IU IM.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **HIV PEP:** **Starting more than 72 hours after injury** – PEP is ineffective after this window; do not start. Also, **forgetting to check renal function before tenofovir** can cause acute kidney injury.
  - **HBV PEP:** **Giving HBIG to someone already immune (anti-HBs >10)** – wasteful; not harmful, but unnecessary.
  - **General:** **Documenting "nil" for follow-up** – failure to arrange occupational health and repeat serology is the most common systemic error.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - PEP side effects: nausea, diarrhoea, headache, fatigue (common, can lead to non-compliance)
  - Drug-induced liver injury (rare, from raltegravir or co-infection)
  - Hypersensitivity reaction (symptoms: fever, rash, myalgia – stop PEP, call ID)
  - Acute HBV or HCV seroconversion (if prophylaxis failed)
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic HBV infection (HBsAg+ for >6 months) – risk of cirrhosis, HCC
  - Chronic HCV infection – risk of cirrhosis, HCC
  - HIV seroconversion – lifelong treatment, stigma, comorbidities
  - Psychological sequelae: post-traumatic stress, anxiety about transmission to partner, fear of career impact
sections.Prognosis:
- **HIV:** PEP reduces seroconversion risk by ~80% if started within 72 hours; overall transmission risk from single needlestick is ~0.3% (higher if deep, high viral load source).
- **HBV:** Without prophylaxis, risk of transmission is up to 30% if source is HBeAg+ (high viral load); with vaccine + HBIG, seroconversion is extremely rare in non-immune.
- **HCV:** No proven PEP; transmission risk ~3%; chronic infection develops in ~75% of those infected (so over 2% of exposed will develop chronic HCV). Early detection (DAA therapy) leads to cure.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Wash the wound immediately with soap and running water** – this single step reduces viral load by >90%. Do not use bleach or alcohol.
- **Always check the source patient’s notes and recent serology** – often they are known positive, and you can decide PEP without waiting for new tests.
- **Start HIV PEP even if the source is unknown if the injury is high-risk** (e.g. deep, bloody hollow-bore needle from a public bin). Obtain a baseline HIV test first, start PEP, and stop later if source tests negative. You cannot restart a missed 72-hour window.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not “defer” PEP while waiting for source test results** – if the injury is high-risk, start PEP immediately; you can stop later if source is negative.
- **Do not forget to check tetanus status** – a needlestick is a tetanus-prone wound; many junior doctors focus only on BBVs.
- **Do not give HIV PEP without baseline HIV test** – you cannot differentiate pre-existing from acquired infection later, which has significant medicolegal consequences.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Any signs of seroconversion illness (fever, rash, lymphadenopathy, jaundice, dark urine, flu-like symptoms) within 6 months of exposure.
- **Return if:** Uncontrolled vomiting or severe drug side effects (especially if unable to keep PEP tablets down – they need alternative antiemetic or referral for IV PEP).
- **Follow-up:** Occupational health appointment within 2 weeks; repeat HIV/HCV serology at 6 weeks, 12 weeks, 6 months. Provide a written plan and a 24-hour contact number (usually occupational health or infectious diseases registrar).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call ID/virology registrar immediately if:**
  - High-risk exposure from a known HIV+ source with high viral load (consider triple drug PEP).
  - Injury >72 hours old but you are unsure about starting PEP (some guidelines allow up to 7 days for HIV PEP if very high risk, but this is controversial).
  - Pregnant clinician exposed – need specialist risk-benefit discussion for PEP.
- **Call senior (consultant/occupational health) if:**
  - Source patient is known to have drug-resistant HIV – alternate PEP regimen needed.
  - Clinician develops severe drug reaction or requires IV PEP.
- **ICU/CRT criteria:** Rarely needed; only if anaphylaxis to PEP or fulminant hepatitis (very rare). Use standard NEWS2 triggers: NEWS2 >5 or rising lactate >4 mmol/L.

rawMarkdown:
# Needlestick Injury & BBV Exposure

**One-liner:** Occupational or accidental puncture with a sharps device contaminated with blood/body fluids, requiring urgent risk assessment and post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) to prevent HIV, HBV, and HCV infection.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Missed 72-hour window for HIV PEP:** The single most critical error. If the injury is high-risk (deep, visible blood, hollow-bore needle from known HIV+ source), delaying PEP beyond 72 hours makes it ineffective. *Finding: injury >72 hours old or source status unknown – do not defer PEP while awaiting results; start empiric PEP if high-risk.*
- **Acute fulminant hepatitis B (rare in first hour but rapid in non-immune):** No immediate killer, but the failure to give HBV vaccine + HBIG within 48 hours can lead to seroconversion and chronic disease. *Finding: non-immune staff (anti-HBs <10 IU/L) with HBsAg+ source needle injury – treat as time-critical (ideally within 24h).*
- **Anaphylaxis to PEP drugs (very rare):** First dose of raltegravir or tenofovir can cause hypersensitivity. *Finding: urticaria, angioedema, hypotension within minutes of first PEP dose – stop drug, call ED.*

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Anxiety, fear, guilt (most prominent – do not dismiss), wound site pain/bleeding, history of sharps injury (time, device type, source patient details)
- **Key exam findings:** Puncture wound (size, depth, location – e.g. finger, palm, through glove), visible blood on needle, wound erythema (if late presentation), check source patient notes if available (HIV/HBV/HCV status, viral loads)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Needlestick injury with BBV exposure** – history of percutaneous injury with blood/body fluid contact; no other plausible cause for the event.
- **Needlestick injury without exposure** – injury from a closed system, sterile needle, or dry blood (non-infectious); differentiate by asking: "Was there visible blood on the needle? Was the source known? Was the needle hollow-bore?"
- **Mucocutaneous exposure** – splash to eyes, mouth, or non-intact skin; same BBV risk but lower transmission probability; different management (no PEP for HIV if low risk, but HBV/HCV same).
- **Splitter:** **Depth of injury + visible blood** – a deep puncture with bloody hollow-bore needle = high risk (treat as PEP-eligible); a superficial scratch with a solid needle (e.g. suture needle) without visible blood = very low risk (no PEP needed, but still test source).

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **Immediate first aid:** Wash wound with soap and water (do not scrub), encourage bleeding (do not suck wound), cover with waterproof dressing.
  - **Assess wound:** depth, location, device type (hollow-bore vs solid), presence of blood.
  - **Source patient risk assessment:** Check notes / lab results for known HIV, HBV, HCV, syphilis. If source unknown (e.g. public sharps bin), treat as high risk.
- **Bloods (baseline for clinician):**
  - **HIV serology** (with consent, document “baseline status”)
  - **HBsAg, anti-HBs, anti-HBc** (to check immunity)
  - **Anti-HCV** (baseline)
  - **Syphilis serology** (if high risk)
  - **LFTs** (baseline for PEP monitoring)
  - **Routine FBC, U&Es** (if PEP to be started)
- **Imaging:** None.
- **Special tests:**
  - **Source patient testing:** Only if they consent – test for HIV (p24 antigen + antibody), HBsAg, HCV antibody. Do not delay PEP awaiting results.
  - **Tetanus status:** Check; give tetanus booster if >10 years since last dose (or tetanus immunoglobulin if high-risk wound + unknown vaccination).
- **Never-miss test:** **Baseline HIV test** – required before starting PEP to avoid treating unrecognised pre-existing infection (false attribution of seroconversion to the injury). **Anti-HBs** – if <10 IU/L, they are non-immune and need HBV PEP.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **First aid** (as above) – do before any other step.
  - **Risk stratification:** Use local policy (e.g. UKAP / NICE algorithm). Key variables:
    - Source: known HIV+ / HBsAg+ / HCV+ or unknown
    - Injury: deep, hollow-bore, visible blood → high risk
    - Clinician immunity: HBV vaccinated (anti-HBs >10) vs non-immune
  - **HIV PEP:** If high risk (deep injury + source HIV+ OR unknown + patient request), start **as soon as possible, within 72 hours**. *Do not wait for source test results if clinical concern.*
    - Drugs: tenofovir disoproxil + emtricitabine (Truvada) 1 tablet daily **plus** raltegravir 400mg twice daily (or dolutegravir 50mg daily) for 28 days. [Check local guideline for exact combination and dosing; some use bictegravir/emtricitabine/tenofovir alafenamide single tablet]
  - **HBV PEP:**
    - If **non-immune** (anti-HBs <10): give **HBIG 500 IU IM** (ideally within 24–48h) **plus** start HBV vaccine (Engerix B 20mcg IM at 0, 1, 2, 6 months).
    - If **immune** (anti-HBs >10): no additional prophylaxis needed.
  - **HCV:** No PEP available. Baseline serology and monitor at 6 weeks, 12 weeks, 6 months.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Admit if:** high-risk injury where PEP compliance is uncertain, severe anxiety, or need for IV PEP (rare – e.g. unable to tolerate oral).
  - **Counselling:** Discuss transmission risks (HIV ~0.3%, HBV ~30% if HBeAg+, HCV ~3%), PEP side effects (nausea, fatigue, renal impairment), and safe sexual practices (condom use, avoid blood donation) until follow-up negative serology.
  - **Follow-up schedule:** HIV test at 6 weeks, 12 weeks, 6 months; HCV test at 6 weeks, 12 weeks, 6 months; LFTs monthly while on PEP (if starting tenofovir, check renal function at baseline and 4 weeks).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Occupational health referral** (mandatory) – they will manage the full follow-up, psychological support, and documentation.
  - **Complete HBV vaccine course** if started (0,1,2,6 months).
  - **If source develops new infection** (e.g. delayed positive HCV), recall exposed clinician for further testing.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: nil specific (normal diet).
  - VTE prophylaxis: not routinely indicated unless immobile.
  - Observations: baseline vital signs only (not for monitoring infection – for PEP side effects if severe).
  - NBM: not required.

### Drugs & Doses
- **HIV PEP (first-line):**
  - Tenofovir disoproxil 245mg + emtricitabine 200mg (Truvada) **1 tablet once daily** **PLUS** raltegravir 400mg **twice daily** (or dolutegravir 50mg **once daily**) for 28 days. [If using a single-tablet regimen like Biktarvy (bictegravir/emtricitabine/tenofovir alafenamide), follow local policy]
  - **Alternative:** If renal impairment (eGFR <60), use tenofovir alafenamide based regimen (e.g. Descovy) – consult pharmacist.
  - **Key contraindications:** Pre-existing renal impairment (check eGFR before starting); pregnancy (raltegravir or dolutegravir are safe, but tenofovir disoproxil can be used with monitoring).
- **HBV PEP:**
  - **Hepatitis B immunoglobulin (HBIG):** 500 IU IM once (within 48h, ideally 24h). No known contraindications.
  - **Hepatitis B vaccine (Engerix B):** 20mcg IM at 0, 1, 2, 6 months.
  - **Contraindications:** Hypersensitivity to yeast; but if non-immune, benefit outweighs risk.
- **Tetanus prophylaxis:**
  - Tetanus vaccine (if >10 years since last booster) – 0.5ml IM.
  - Tetanus immunoglobulin (if wound high-risk + unknown vaccination) – 250 IU IM.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **HIV PEP:** **Starting more than 72 hours after injury** – PEP is ineffective after this window; do not start. Also, **forgetting to check renal function before tenofovir** can cause acute kidney injury.
  - **HBV PEP:** **Giving HBIG to someone already immune (anti-HBs >10)** – wasteful; not harmful, but unnecessary.
  - **General:** **Documenting "nil" for follow-up** – failure to arrange occupational health and repeat serology is the most common systemic error.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - PEP side effects: nausea, diarrhoea, headache, fatigue (common, can lead to non-compliance)
  - Drug-induced liver injury (rare, from raltegravir or co-infection)
  - Hypersensitivity reaction (symptoms: fever, rash, myalgia – stop PEP, call ID)
  - Acute HBV or HCV seroconversion (if prophylaxis failed)
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic HBV infection (HBsAg+ for >6 months) – risk of cirrhosis, HCC
  - Chronic HCV infection – risk of cirrhosis, HCC
  - HIV seroconversion – lifelong treatment, stigma, comorbidities
  - Psychological sequelae: post-traumatic stress, anxiety about transmission to partner, fear of career impact

### Prognosis
- **HIV:** PEP reduces seroconversion risk by ~80% if started within 72 hours; overall transmission risk from single needlestick is ~0.3% (higher if deep, high viral load source).
- **HBV:** Without prophylaxis, risk of transmission is up to 30% if source is HBeAg+ (high viral load); with vaccine + HBIG, seroconversion is extremely rare in non-immune.
- **HCV:** No proven PEP; transmission risk ~3%; chronic infection develops in ~75% of those infected (so over 2% of exposed will develop chronic HCV). Early detection (DAA therapy) leads to cure.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Wash the wound immediately with soap and running water** – this single step reduces viral load by >90%. Do not use bleach or alcohol.
- **Always check the source patient’s notes and recent serology** – often they are known positive, and you can decide PEP without waiting for new tests.
- **Start HIV PEP even if the source is unknown if the injury is high-risk** (e.g. deep, bloody hollow-bore needle from a public bin). Obtain a baseline HIV test first, start PEP, and stop later if source tests negative. You cannot restart a missed 72-hour window.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not “defer” PEP while waiting for source test results** – if the injury is high-risk, start PEP immediately; you can stop later if source is negative.
- **Do not forget to check tetanus status** – a needlestick is a tetanus-prone wound; many junior doctors focus only on BBVs.
- **Do not give HIV PEP without baseline HIV test** – you cannot differentiate pre-existing from acquired infection later, which has significant medicolegal consequences.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Any signs of seroconversion illness (fever, rash, lymphadenopathy, jaundice, dark urine, flu-like symptoms) within 6 months of exposure.
- **Return if:** Uncontrolled vomiting or severe drug side effects (especially if unable to keep PEP tablets down – they need alternative antiemetic or referral for IV PEP).
- **Follow-up:** Occupational health appointment within 2 weeks; repeat HIV/HCV serology at 6 weeks, 12 weeks, 6 months. Provide a written plan and a 24-hour contact number (usually occupational health or infectious diseases registrar).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call ID/virology registrar immediately if:**
  - High-risk exposure from a known HIV+ source with high viral load (consider triple drug PEP).
  - Injury >72 hours old but you are unsure about starting PEP (some guidelines allow up to 7 days for HIV PEP if very high risk, but this is controversial).
  - Pregnant clinician exposed – need specialist risk-benefit discussion for PEP.
- **Call senior (consultant/occupational health) if:**
  - Source patient is known to have drug-resistant HIV – alternate PEP regimen needed.
  - Clinician develops severe drug reaction or requires IV PEP.
- **ICU/CRT criteria:** Rarely needed; only if anaphylaxis to PEP or fulminant hepatitis (very rare). Use standard NEWS2 triggers: NEWS2 >5 or rising lactate >4 mmol/L.
